title: Cross-Platform UI Framework (Generic)
description: Build mobile/desktop applications with single codebase using cross-platform UI frameworks. This generic pattern helps you choose between Flutter, React Native, Xamarin, and other options based on team skills and requirements.

cost.costNotes: Primary costs are development labor, not infrastructure. Choose framework based on team skills and target platforms.

provenance.estimatedMonthlyRangeUsd: Cross-platform UI frameworks run on client devices with minimal infrastructure costs. Min=$0 represents pure mobile apps (only $25-99/year app store fees, amortized to ~$2-8/mo). Max=$150 accounts for: Apple Developer ($99/yr = $8/mo) + Google Play ($25 one-time = $2/mo amortized) + web deployment with CDN/hosting ($20-50/mo for Netlify/Vercel Pro) + basic analytics/crash reporting ($30-50/mo for Firebase/Sentry) + CI/CD pipeline costs ($20-40/mo for Codemagic/Bitrise for mobile builds). Desktop-only apps have $0/mo infrastructure cost.
provenance.adoptionCost: High complexity due to: (1) Framework-specific learning curve - Flutter requires Dart proficiency (2-3 weeks), React Native needs React + native bridge understanding (2-4 weeks), Xamarin requires C#/.NET (1-3 weeks). (2) Platform-specific nuances - iOS/Android differences in navigation, permissions, and UI paradigms require 1-2 weeks to master. (3) Build toolchain setup - Xcode, Android Studio, platform SDKs, signing certificates (1 week). (4) Testing strategy across platforms (1 week). With AI coding assistants, this reduces from ~6-8 weeks to 4-6 weeks for a proficient developer ($150/hr × 160 hours = $24,000 traditional; with AI assistance: $150/hr × 100 hours = $15,000). For team adoption: $3,500 represents training, initial proof-of-concept, and tooling setup for a small team (2-3 developers, 1-2 weeks part-time).
provenance.licenseCost: All major cross-platform frameworks are open source with permissive licenses: Flutter (BSD), React Native (MIT), Xamarin/.NET MAUI (MIT). Platform development tools are also free: Android Studio (Apache 2.0), Xcode (free with macOS). Only recurring costs are app store registration fees ($99/yr Apple, $25 one-time Google) which are operational, not license costs. No commercial IDE licenses required (VS Code is free). Optional enterprise support contracts exist but are not mandatory for framework usage.

provides.reasoning: This is the core capability of the pattern - enabling UI development that works across multiple platforms (iOS, Android, Web, Desktop) from a single codebase.
Cross-platform frameworks like Flutter and React Native primarily target mobile platforms, making this a fundamental provided capability.
Many modern cross-platform frameworks (Flutter, Electron, React Native for Windows/macOS) now support desktop platforms as first-class targets.
Several cross-platform frameworks support web deployment (Flutter Web, React Native Web), though this may vary by framework choice and maturity.
Cross-platform frameworks enable creation of reusable UI components that work across all targeted platforms, providing consistent user experiences.
Business logic can be written once and shared across all platforms, reducing duplication and maintenance overhead.
Most modern cross-platform frameworks offer hot-reload or live-reload capabilities for rapid development iteration without full recompilation.
Modern cross-platform frameworks use declarative UI paradigms (Flutter widgets, React components) for building user interfaces.
The pattern provides abstraction over platform-specific differences, allowing developers to write platform-agnostic code while accessing native features when needed.

requires.reasoning: Cross-platform frameworks require native platform toolchains (Android SDK, Xcode, etc.) to compile and package applications for their target platforms.
A build system is essential for compiling cross-platform code, managing platform-specific builds, and generating deployable artifacts.
Package managers are required for managing framework dependencies, third-party libraries, and maintaining consistent development environments.
Access to native platform APIs is necessary for platform-specific features, device capabilities, and optimal user experiences that go beyond framework abstractions.

supports_nfr.reason: Client-side framework availability limited by device/browser issues and OS/platform compatibility.
Mobile deployments via app stores take 24-48hrs for review; web deployments achieve faster RTO.
Cross-platform UI frameworks support GDPR-compliant implementations through data minimization and user controls.
Cross-platform UI frameworks can implement HIPAA-compliant applications with proper encryption and access controls.
Cross-platform UI frameworks support CCPA compliance through user data management and deletion capabilities.
Cross-platform UI frameworks can enforce SOX compliance through audit trails and access controls.

supports_constraints.reason: Cross-platform UI frameworks are cloud-agnostic and run on any infrastructure.
Cross-platform UI frameworks target mobile (iOS/Android), desktop (Windows/macOS/Linux), and web platforms.
This pattern supports specific cross-platform UI frameworks: Flutter (Dart), React Native (JavaScript/TypeScript), Xamarin (C#), and native bridges.
Cross-platform UI frameworks support optional client-side caching for offline-first and performance optimization.
Cross-platform UI frameworks can integrate with async messaging for real-time updates via WebSockets or push notifications.
Cross-platform UI frameworks support on-device AI inference through native ML libraries and platform APIs.
Cross-platform UI frameworks support multi-tenant applications through user context management and backend integration.
Cross-platform UI frameworks can trigger and display batch processing results but delegate execution to backend.
Cross-platform UI frameworks coordinate distributed transactions through backend APIs and optimistic UI patterns.
Cross-platform UI frameworks support real-time streaming through WebSockets, SSE, and platform-specific push APIs.
Cross-platform UI frameworks can display vector search results and integrate with vector databases via backend APIs.
Cross-platform UI frameworks interact with document stores through backend APIs and can cache documents locally.
Cross-platform UI frameworks use key-value stores for local storage and session management via platform APIs.
Cross-platform UI frameworks can visualize graph data and query graph databases through backend services.
Cross-platform UI frameworks render time-series data visualizations and charts from backend time-series databases.
Cross-platform UI frameworks support OLTP workloads through transactional forms and real-time data updates.
Cross-platform UI frameworks support OLAP workloads through analytics dashboards and data visualization components.

framework.description: Cross-platform framework choice. Flutter: fast rendering, single codebase. React Native: web developer friendly, large ecosystem. Xamarin/.NET MAUI: enterprise C# integration. Kotlin Multiplatform: native performance with shared business logic.

target_platforms.description: Target platforms for deployment. More platforms increase maintenance complexity but maximize reach.

state_management.description: State management approach. Provider: simple, built-in. BLoC: structured, testable. Redux: predictable, verbose. MobX: reactive, less boilerplate. Riverpod: compile-safe provider evolution.

native_integration.description: Level of platform-specific native code integration. Minimal: pure cross-platform. Moderate: some native modules. Extensive: heavy native customization (increases platform-specific maintenance).

ui_approach.description: UI rendering strategy. Custom-rendering: consistent look across platforms (Flutter). Native-components: platform-native feel (React Native). Hybrid: mix of both approaches.
